profile.summary: A Senior Engineer owns complex delivery end to end, makes key technical decisions, and raises the quality bar for the team. They work closely with product, design, and other engineers, balancing hands-on implementation with system design, code review, incident response, and mentoring less experienced teammates.

known_challenges.text: Balancing deep hands-on work with the expectation to guide others and unblock the team.
Carrying informal leadership without always having clear authority over priorities or technical direction.
Keeping delivery moving while protecting code quality, maintainability, and technical debt work.
Context switching across feature work, reviews, support, and production issues can drain focus.
Being seen as the person who should handle the hardest problems can create sustained pressure.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Tradeoffs between delivery speed and technical quality
recommended_question_themes.why: Senior Engineers are often where short-term product pressure meets long-term engineering health.
This level often succeeds or stalls based on how clearly they can own problems and influence direction.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Mentoring and raising the bar for the team
recommended_question_themes.why: Their impact is usually broader than individual output, especially through reviews, pairing, and guidance.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Load from interruptions, support work, and hard problem ownership
recommended_question_themes.why: Senior Engineers often absorb invisible work that affects energy and sustained performance.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Growth from strong implementer to technical leader
recommended_question_themes.why: This level is often preparing for wider architectural and cross-team influence, not just more coding.

terminology.meaning: Planning how parts of a system fit and scale.
Checking code changes for quality, clarity, and risk.
Shortcuts that make future changes slower or riskier.
Improving code structure without changing behavior.
Logs, metrics, and traces that explain system behavior.
A production problem affecting users or system health.
Helping other engineers grow through guidance and feedback.
Discussion to test a technical approach before building.
A meaningful technical choice with lasting impact.
Removing obstacles so others can keep moving.
The engineering approach a team is aligning around.
Working closely with product, design, and other partners.
A short delivery cycle with planned work.
The ordered list of work still to do.
Two engineers working together on the same problem.
Being responsible for responding to production issues.

listen_for: Whether they talk about team outcomes or only their own tickets.
How they describe tradeoffs between shipping quickly and building sustainably.
Whether production issues and support work are taking over planned engineering time.
How often they mention mentoring, reviews, and unblocking as part of their week.
Whether they sound clear or frustrated about who makes technical decisions.
avoid: Treating them like they should just code faster instead of handling broader technical responsibilities.
Assuming informal leadership means they have authority to set priorities on their own.
Pushing all hard or messy work toward them because they are reliable.
Judging impact only by visible feature output and ignoring mentoring, reviews, and system health work.
